Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 83 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the state now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The state didn't change! The block is still at (24, 30) and the blob is still at (26, 32) with 65 pixels. It seems like clicking at (25, 31) didn't move the block because it was already at (24, 30) and (25, 31) is too close (or the block can't move that direction).
Wait, actually looking at the previous state, the block was at (24, 30) and I clicked at (25, 31). The block should have moved towards (25, 31), but it stayed at (24, 30). This is strange.
Maybe the block can only move in certain directions, or there's a minimum distance requirement for the click to be effective.
Let me try clicking further away from the block, like at (26, 33), to move it towards the blob.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions trying to understand the game mechanics. I need to commit to a hypothesis and test it efficiently.
Hypothesis: The goal is to move all 4 magenta blocks to surround/absorb the blue blob. Each block needs to be moved close to the blob to shrink it.
Let me try a more aggressive approach: move all 4 blocks towards the blob in a batch of actions. I'll click at positions around the blob to attract each block.
Current block positions: (24, 30), (40, 36), (56, 46), (56, 16)
Blob position: (26, 32)
Let me try moving each block closer to the blob by clicking at positions between the block and the blob.
For block at (24, 30): click at (25, 31) - but this didn't work. Let me try (26, 32) which is the blob's center.
For block at (40, 36): click at (33, 34) which is between the block and blob.
For block at (56, 46): click at (41, 39) which is between the block and blob.
For block at (56, 16): click at (41, 24) which is between the block and blob.
Let me try this batch.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving all 4 blocks towards the blob in one batch
# Block positions: (24, 30), (40, 36), (56, 46), (56, 16)
# Blob position: (26, 32)
actions = [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 32},  # Move block at (24, 30) to blob center
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 34},  # Move block at (40, 36) towards blob
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 39},  # Move block at (56, 46) towards blob
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 24},  # Move block at (56, 16) towards blob
]
action(actions)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
